summary:
The condition arises because there’s an abnormality or defect within the Low-Density Lipoprotein Receptor (LDL-R) pathway leading to its dysfunction. This genetic mutation impairs cells' ability effectively remove circulating low-density lipoproteins resulting in elevated blood levels indicative of high cholesterol conditions seen clinically associated with ADH Type II. Therefore,
Autosomal Dominant Hypercholesterolemia type II involves
a problem with \textbf{\underline{the LDL-receptor}} function. To summarize Agent1's reasoning into two sentences focusing only on key clinical points:

Autosomal dominant hypercholesterolemia type II occurs due to defective Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) receptors, impairing their normal role in clearing LDL cholesterol from the bloodstream. Consequently, patients exhibit persistently raised serum cholesterol levels characteristic of this disorder. Thus, 
The cause is a malfunction of the \textbf{\underline{Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) receptor}}.
